coredata.dc:title: Utilization of carbon dots from jackfruit for real-time sensing of acetone vapor and understanding the electronic and interfacial interactions using density functional theory
coredata.dc:creator: Thongsai, Nichaphat
Jaiyong, Panichakorn
Kladsomboon, Sumana
In, Insik
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are emerging carbon-based nanomaterials because of their unique optical properties, high surface area, and surface chelating functional groups. In this work, carbon dots were prepared from jackfruit using a one-step hydrothermal method and used as a sensing layer in an optical electronic nose for the real-time detection of acetone vapor at room temperature. The carbon dots showed blue photoluminescence with excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, excellent photostability, and a quantum yield of 5.2%. Using principal component analysis, the carbon dot-integrated electronic nose was able to distinguish acetone from hexane, ethanol, methanol, and water and between different concentrations of acetone in ethanol and aqueous solutions. From the time-dependent density functional theory calculations, an increase in carbon dot's extinction coefficients in bulk solvents was in a good agreement with the optical electronic nose results. The calculations of interaction energy using density functional theory method illustrated the electronic coupling and interfacial interactions between carbon dots and acetone and other volatile organic compounds. Interestingly, the unique ambipolar properties of carbon dots were computationally demonstrated. Furthermore, the photoluminescence of carbon dots was also exploited for the detection of acetone in aqueous solutions. Based on this work, our jackfruit-derived carbon dots were demonstrated to be versatile sensing materials for acetone in vapor and solution, and the computational methods highlighted the importance of interfacial electronic coupling towards unique sensing properties of carbon dots.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dot from jackfruit
Acetone vapor detection using optical electronic nose
Calculation of absorption spectra using time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT)
Calculation of interaction energy between carbon dots and VOCs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis and application of carbon dots from jackfruit.
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) survey XPS spectrum, and high-resolution spectra of (c) C1s, (d) N1s, and (e) O1s of carbon dots.
(a) HR-TEM images of and (b) size distribution histogram of carbon dots measured from the HR-TEM images.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of carbon dots in aqueous solution. Inset images are photographs of carbon dot solution under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV irradiation (right), (b) excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra of the carbon dots, (c) relative absorbance and fluorescence emission intensity of 0.05 g·L−1 carbon dot solution after exposure to UV irradiation, and (d) relative fluorescence emission intensity of 0.05 g·L−1 carbon dot solution under visible light.

(a) Sensor signals of carbon dot film under violet light (399 nm), (b) the comparison between gas sensing response (S) of carbon dot film towards different solvents under eight light sources, and (c) PCA two-dimensional score plots for hexane (red), acetone (purple), ethanol (blue), methanol (yellow), and water (green). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Sensor signals of carbon dot film, (b) PCA two-dimensional score plots for acetone/water solutions, (c) sensor signals of carbon dot film, (d) PCA two-dimensional score plots for acetone/ethanol solutions.

(a) Top-view and side-view of optimized structure of TD-DFT model for carbon dots, (b) the simulated absorption spectra of carbon dot in different solvents, and top-view and side-view of optimized models: (a) ethanol‑carbon dot, (b) methanol‑carbon dot, (c) acetone‑carbon dot, and (d) hexane‑carbon dot complexes.

Molecular orbitals of (a) pristine graphene fragment, (b) carbon dot, (c) acetone‑carbon dot complex, (d) phenol‑carbon dot complex, and (e) phenol dimers. Green/red indicates different phases of p-orbitals. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra and (b) intensities of carbon dots in acetone/water solutions at different concentrations.
Model of the change in optical properties of carbon dots in the presence of VOCs.
Calculated interaction energies (I.E.) between carbon dots and VOCs.

head.ce:title: Utilization of carbon dots from jackfruit for real-time sensing of acetone vapor and understanding the electronic and interfacial interactions using density functional theory

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12121, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiological Technology, Faculty of Medical Technology, Mahidol University, Nakhon Pathom, 73170, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of IT Convergence (Brain Korea PLUS 21), Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of IT Convergence (Brain Korea PLUS 21)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are emerging carbon-based nanomaterials because of their unique optical properties, high surface area, and surface chelating functional groups. In this work, carbon dots were prepared from jackfruit using a one-step hydrothermal method and used as a sensing layer in an optical electronic nose for the real-time detection of acetone vapor at room temperature. The carbon dots showed blue photoluminescence with excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, excellent photostability, and a quantum yield of 5.2%. Using principal component analysis, the carbon dot-integrated electronic nose was able to distinguish acetone from hexane, ethanol, methanol, and water and between different concentrations of acetone in ethanol and aqueous solutions. From the time-dependent density functional theory calculations, an increase in carbon dot's extinction coefficients in bulk solvents was in a good agreement with the optical electronic nose results. The calculations of interaction energy using density functional theory method illustrated the electronic coupling and interfacial interactions between carbon dots and acetone and other volatile organic compounds. Interestingly, the unique ambipolar properties of carbon dots were computationally demonstrated. Furthermore, the photoluminescence of carbon dots was also exploited for the detection of acetone in aqueous solutions. Based on this work, our jackfruit-derived carbon dots were demonstrated to be versatile sensing materials for acetone in vapor and solution, and the computational methods highlighted the importance of interfacial electronic coupling towards unique sensing properties of carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots, also known as carbon quantum dots or carbon nanodots, are optically stable luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials exhibiting strong light absorption and emission. Carbon dots are rich in carbon, and therefore can ideally be synthesized from any precursors that contain carbon atoms, preferably ones with a high carbon content. They have been prepared from natural materials [1–4], wastes [5–7], and chemical precursors [8–10] using straightforward thermal treatments, including solvothermal, pyrolysis, and hydrothermal methods, normally with the aid of acids as catalysts/dehydrating agents [11–13]. Through acid oxidation during the synthesis, hydrophilic functional groups, including hydroxyl, amino, and carboxylate groups are formed on the surface of the carbon dots. In addition to making carbon dots highly soluble in aqueous solutions, these functional groups are believed to be emissive sites responsible for the unique optical properties [14]. Furthermore, they can be functionalized to introduce new functions, and, more importantly, have strong interactions with metal ions and polar molecules [9]. Upon adsorption of these chemicals onto the surface, the optical properties of carbon dots are altered, providing selectivity and sensitivity that endow the carbon dots to be used as chemical and bio-probes [15–17].

Recently, the synthesis of carbon dots revolves around the use of readily available, low cost natural resources as precursors. In this work, we synthesized carbon dots from fresh local jackfruit using the hydrothermal method and used them as a sensing material to detect acetone in vapor and solution. From a few previous studies, carbon dots have demonstrated that their photoluminescence emission is dependent upon interactions with acetone molecules. For example, Cayuela et al. observed the fluorescence enhancement when the carbon dots, derived from multi-walled carbon nanotubes, were passivated with acetone [18]. Das et al. reported the fluorescent quenching of heteroatom-doped carbon dots, prepared from kappa carrageenan and urea, upon exposure to acetone in human fluids [19]. Liu et al. demonstrated the fluorescence emission enhancement of carbon dots with the addition of acetone vapor; however, the sensitivity and selectivity were not demonstrated [20]. We therefore made use of optical absorptivity of carbon dots to sense acetone vapor and investigated their sensitivity and selectivity. When the optical absorptivity of carbon dots is employed for sensing, advantageously there is no need to maximize the photoluminescence quantum yields of carbon dots by carrying out tedious synthesis procedures or finding suitable precursors. In addition, there is often batch-to-batch variation in quantum yields of carbon dots, especially of those prepared from natural precursors, but the absorbance of carbon dots is much less affected. Recently, we developed an electronic nose system integrated with carbon dots for the detection of alcohol and formic acid vapors by monitoring a change of optical absorbance of the carbon dots [16,21,22]. Therefore, we aimed to extend further by using the carbon dot-integrated electronic nose for detecting acetone vapors and several volatile organic compounds (VOCs). Identification and quantification of VOCs are of interest for safety and quality control and environmental monitoring. Acetone is one of the most widely used organic solvents in the chemical, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic industries and research laboratories. It is a highly water-soluble, volatile organic solvent and can be harmful to human body. It is also highly flammable and therefore dangerous when there is a leakage. Its low vapor pressure and harmful nature make it toxic and hazardous. High concentrations of or prolonged exposure to acetone can cause skin dryness, dermatitis, irritation, headaches, fatigue, nausea, liver and kidney malfunction, and possible death when the concentration exceeds 1000 ppm [20,23]. In addition to safety monitoring, acetone vapor detection is beneficial for health diagnostic purposes. Patients with diabetes, obesity, or high blood sugar, tend to have a high concentration of acetone in their breath [24]. Therefore, identification and quantification of acetone will benefit a range of chemical and biomedical fields. Analytical methods of acetone vapor detection include gas chromatography, mass spectrometry, and the use of electronic sensors. However, gas chromatography and mass spectrometry require pretreatment of gases [25]. Electronic sensors are promising alternative because of their compact size, convenience, and precision. A number of acetone sensors have been fabricated using a variety of sensing materials, including semiconductors and chemoluminescent/luminescent and piezoelectric materials [26–29]. Among them, graphene, carbon nanotube, metal oxides, and metal chalcogenides have been widely investigated [30–33]. Despite their high sensitivity and selectivity, these carbon-based nanomaterials require tedious preparation procedures and some are susceptible to oxidation under ambient operating conditions. Metal oxide-based sensors are easier to prepare than their carbon-based counterparts, but require high operating temperatures and the addition of dopants to increase sensitivity. Moreover, the commonly used metal oxides like ZnO and SnO2 are prone to acid or base degradation [34–36]. Carbon dots are thus an excellent alternative choice for acetone vapor detection because of their straightforward preparation and high surface area, polarity, and chemical stability.

Specifically, we integrated a jackfruit-derived carbon dot thin film into an optical electronic nose for the real-time detection of acetone vapor at room temperature. Our electronic nose is capable of detecting a change in optical absorbance of a material film when exposed to vapors. The electronic nose is a device that mimics the human nose and is widely used to detect VOCs. It has been used for safety and quality control in environmental and health monitoring and in the food industries [37–39]. In our system, upon adsorption and desorption of the analyte gases, the change in light transmission through the carbon dot film was converted to an electrical signal, providing a pattern recognition profile which was further analyzed using principal component analysis (PCA). We were able to fabricate a carbon dot-based sensor that could identify and quantify acetone vapor in aqueous and ethanol solutions, and distinguish acetone vapor from other common VOCs. Our optically unique carbon dots are a promising sensor for acetone, and potentially for other VOCs. We also exploited the photoluminescence of the carbon dots to quantify the acetone in the solutions in order to demonstrate the versatility of the carbon dots.

In addition to extensive experimental studies, density functional theory (DFT) calculation was performed to investigate interfacial interactions between carbon dots and VOCs. Despite being a hot topic of research, thousands of publications have focused on experimental details and most theoretical studies have concentrated on photophysics of carbon dots, i.e., HOMO-LUMO gap and origin of photoluminescence, of various carbon dot models [40–42]. Our work is thus a rare example for which the computational methods were used to provide valuable insights on the carbon dot sensing abilities. Recently, Kunaseth et al. studied the adsorption of VOCs on transition metal-doped graphene using DFT methods and reported intriguing computational results [43]. Xue et al. found that the carbon dot-phenol formed the most stable hydrogen bond model as a complex, leading to sensitive detection [44]. We made use the nitrogen-doped graphitic fragment with oxygen-containing functional groups at the edge to represent the carbon dot. For the first time, the interaction energies between the carbon dot and VOCs were calculated and agreed well with the electronic nose results. The inclusion of implicit solvent effects was also investigated by time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) methodology and show consistency with experimental results. Interestingly, the frontier molecular orbital calculations demonstrated that the carbon dot can act as electron donor and acceptor, showing their potentials for ambipolar electronic and optoelectronic devices. Our work is therefore a good example that straightforward computational methods can not only elucidate the unique sensing abilities of carbon dots but also provide profound understandings and will lead to better design of functional, efficient carbon dots for future applications.

All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich unless otherwise noted and used without further purification. 65% Nitric acid (HNO3)- and sodium hydroxide (NaOH)-reagent grade were obtained from Carlo Erba Reagents. High-grade regenerated cellulose dialysis membrane (MWCO 1000 Da) was bought from Spectrum Labs. Deionized (DI) water with resistance of 15 MΩ was used throughout. Fresh jackfruit was purchased from a local supermarket in Pathumthani, Thailand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dot from jackfruit
ce:section.ce:para: The fresh, crushed jackfruit (20 g) was mixed with 2.5 M nitric acid and sonicated for 15 min. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h using a hot-air oven. After the reaction, the mixture turned brown, and was then allowed to cool to room temperature. The mixture was diluted with DI water and filtered through cellulose paper to remove the residue. The water-soluble filtrate was then neutralized using a 10 M NaOH solution. The crude carbon dot solution was extensively dialyzed against DI water through dialysis membranes (MWCO 1000 Da) for three days, and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the supernatant was freeze dried for 24 h to yield brown carbon dot powder (0.057 g, 1.05%).

The optical properties, including absorption and emission, of the carbon dot aqueous solution (0.05 g·L−1) were investigated. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-1700 PharmaSpec). A fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco, FP-6200) was used to measure the fluorescence emission spectrum. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained using a Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer (Spectrum 2000) with KBr pellets. The elemental composition of the carbon dots was determined using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS; AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos analytical, Manchester, UK). The sample was excited with X-ray hybrid mode at 700 × 300 cm2 spot area with monochromatic Al Kα1,2 radiation at 1.4 keV. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained from a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI Corporation) microscope. The zeta potential of the carbon dots was measured using a Nano Particle Analyzer (HORIBA, Nano Partica SZ-100) at 25 °C with a scattering angle of 173°. The time-resolved photoluminescence of the carbon dots was measured using an FL920 fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments).

The quantum yield of the carbon dots was determined using UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometry with quinine sulfate as the reference. The carbon dots were dissolved in DI water (n = 1.33), whereas the quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid (quantum yield 54%, n = 1.33, at 360 nm). The quantum yield was calculated using Eq. (1) [45]:
(1)
Φ carbon dots
=
Φ reference
×
m carbon dots
m reference
×
ɳ carbon dots 2
ɳ reference 2
where Φ is the quantum yield, m is slope between integrated fluorescence emission intensity and UV–vis absorbance, and ɳ is the refractive index of solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acetone vapor detection using optical electronic nose
ce:section.ce:para: A carbon dot thin film was prepared on a cleaned 2.5 × 2.5 cm glass slide by drop-casting the carbon dot aqueous solution (1 mL, 20 g·L−1) in DI water, which had been ultrasonicated for at least 20 min. The film was then allowed to dry at 70 °C overnight. The detection of VOCs using carbon dots as sensing materials was performed by home-made optical electronic nose system. The details of the electronic nose setup can be found in our previous work [22]. The measurement of the dynamic gas flow system was carried out by switching between the sample gases for 1 min and the N2 for 2 min using solenoid valve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of absorption spectra using time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT)
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to our previous study [22], the absorption spectra of the graphitic fragment substituted with nitrogen atoms and oxygen-containing groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) at the edge (C35H16N4O9) in the presence of VOCs were computed. The TD-DFT/M06-2X/def2-TZVPP/PCM (polarizable continuum model) method was used to calculate the first 20 lowest excited states, which were then used to generate the absorption spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of interaction energy between carbon dots and VOCs
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, a graphitic fragment substituted with nitrogen atoms and oxygen-containing groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) at the edge (C52H18N5O9) was used as the model for carbon dots. The molecule of VOCs: methanol, ethanol, acetone, and hexane was placed on top; and the dimer geometries were initially optimized using semiempirical Hamiltonian of PM6-D3H4 method [46] in MOPAC2016 (Version: 17.361 L). Subsequently, the dimers were reoptimized using M06-2X density functionals with def2-SVP basis set [47]. The interaction energy (I.E.) between the carbon dot and VOCs was computed at the M06-2X/def2-TZVPP level with the corrected basis set superposition error, based on Eq. (2):
(2)
I . E . =
E complex
−
E
carbon
dot
+
E
VO C
where E
complex is the total energy of carbon dot-VOC complex, E
carbon dot is the energy of carbon dot, and E
VOC is the energy of VOC. All calculations were carried out using the Gaussian 09 program package [48].

Jackfruit (Artocarpus heterophyllus Lam.) is a traditional fruit grew and consumed in many countries. It is a cheap and readily available carbon source that is rich in carbohydrates, sugars, proteins, fibers, and a variety of minerals and vitamins, including calcium, potassium, sodium, vitamin A, thiamine, and riboflavin [49]. The fresh, crushed jackfruit was subjected to the hydrothermal process at 200 °C for 6 h for the preparation of carbon dots. In the presence of nitric acid as a dehydrating agent, and at high pressure and temperature, the fruit underwent dehydration/hydrolysis and carbonization to produce the carbon dots. After centrifuging to remove large non-fluorescent residues, the resulting solution was purified using dialysis and freeze-dried to yield carbon dots (Fig. 1 ). The obtained carbon dots were blue luminescence under 365-nm excitation. Other reaction temperatures and times yielded carbon dots with much lower photoluminescence, and therefore, the carbon dots hydrothermal-treated at 200 °C for 6 h were used for the rest of experiments.

To analyze the surface functional groups and elemental components of the carbon dots, FT-IR and XPS analysis were carried out. As shown in Fig. 2a, the FT-IR spectrum showed the signals of OH stretching vibration at 3423 cm−1, and CH stretching vibration at 2922 cm−1. The signals at 1596 and 1404 cm−1 corresponded to the vibrational modes of CO/CC stretching and CH bending. These results confirmed the existence of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of the carbon dots, which endow them with high aqueous solubility. These functional groups can induce negative charges on the surface of carbon dots, which is consistent with the zeta potential of −52.1 mV. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) also verified the presence of oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon atoms at atomic concentrations of 23.97%, 3.08%, and 64.43%, respectively. High resolution analysis of the C1s peak (Fig. 2c) confirmed the existence of graphitic and oxygenated carbon with signals of CC/CC at 284.9 eV, CO/CN at 286.8 eV, CO at 287.8 eV, and OCO at 288.9 eV. High resolution analysis of the N1s peak (Fig. 2d) showed the presence of pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen atoms at 398.3 eV, 399.9 eV, and 401.7 eV, respectively. High resolution of the O1s peak (Fig. 2e) revealed signals of CO, CO, and OCO at 531.4 eV, 531.9 eV, and 533.6 eV, respectively. The FT-IR and XPS results confirmed that the carbon dots contained graphitic carbon and carbonyl/carboxylate groups, which accounted for the negatively charged surface and high water-solubility of the carbon dots.

The size and morphology of the carbon dots were characterized using HR-TEM. The HR-TEM images (Fig. 3a and b) showed quasi-spherical carbon dot particles with parallel graphitic lines. The d-spacing of about 0.3 nm, which is close to that of the graphite crystal structure, corresponds to (002) plane of graphitic carbon [2]. This suggested the presence of conjugation within the core of the carbon dots. The size distribution histogram (Fig. 3c) obtained from the HR-TEM image showed a range of 3–6 nm with an average size of around 5.4 nm.

The optical properties of the carbon dots were investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibited a broad peak at about 250–280 nm, which was attributed to π–π⁎ transition of aromatic sp2 domains [50] (Fig. 4a). The long absorption tail up to 550 nm is due to low energy transitions within the surface states, created by the surface functional groups [51]. This is consistent with the FT-IR and HR-TEM results, which showed the presence of conjugation. The maximum fluorescence emission spectrum was observed at 479 nm when using the maximum excitation wavelength of 374 nm. The images inset in Fig. 4a show that the carbon dot solution was light yellow under visible light and blue under UV irradiation at 365 nm. Carbon dots of about 5 nm in size are usually blue emitters when excited by UV and short wavelengths in visible range [52,53]. The quantum yield was calculated to be 5.2%, which is high for unmodified carbon dots and comparable with carbon dots synthesized from other fruits such as carbon dot from mango (quantum yields ~0.5–4%) [13], and carbon dot from coconut (quantum yield ~2.8%) [54]. The emission exhibited excitation-dependence (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence emission intensity dropped sequentially as the excitation wavelength was increased. Due to the presence of conjugation, crystalline graphitic domains, and various surface functional groups, the photoluminescence and excitation dependence emission of carbon dots are responsible by a combination of both quantum confinement effect and emissive surface states [55,56]. The time-resolved photoluminescence of the carbon dots was investigated using an excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The photoluminescence was found to decay exponentially. The decay curves were then fitted using a double-exponential mathematical function (Fig. S1, and Table S1, Supplementary Information). The multiple lifetimes (2.1 and 7.7 ns) might be due to the presence of various fluorophores and/or the energy levels of the emissive units, leading to a broad absorption and emission range as observed in UV–vis and photoluminescence spectra [57].

A photobleaching experiment was carried out by measuring the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission of the carbon dots under continuous UV irradiation (24 W, 365 nm). The fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorbance remained above 90% over 6 h of irradiation. This confirmed that our jackfruit-derived carbon dots are essentially photostable (Fig. 4c). The long-term stability or shelf life of the carbon dot solution was next investigated (Fig. 4d). We found that the fluorescence emission intensity of the carbon dot solution remained above 90% after 6 weeks. These photostability experiments confirmed that our carbon dots have sufficient stability for practical applications.

To be practical, carbon dots should maintain their structural and optical properties in various environments. We first tested the photostability of the carbon dots in ionic solutions, as these are a standard working environment in many carbon dot applications. First, the fluorescence emission intensity in sodium chloride solution (0–2 M) was measured (Fig. S2a and b, Supplementary Information). Neither significant difference in fluorescence emission intensity nor aggregation was observed within the tested concentration range, consistent with previous report [51]. The pH-dependence photoluminescence of carbon dots was investigated (Fig. S2c and d, Supplementary Information). The photoluminescence was found to be stable in the pH range of 3–11 with the maximum intensity at pH of 5. Although the highly acidic (pH < 3) and basic (pH > 11) conditions possibly led to the destruction of some emissive states/sites [58], the photoluminescence emission intensity remained above 50% even under pH = 1 and 13. This suggested that our carbon dots are suitable and practical for use in working environments or harsh conditions.

Carbon dots have strong UV–visible absorption and hydrophilic functional groups on the surface and excellent stability under various conditions. They are therefore excellent probes for small molecules, especially polar ones. We next used the carbon dot film, prepared by simple drop-casting method, as a probe in an optical electronic nose for VOC sensing. Our optical electronic nose system comprised LED light sources, a photodetector, and a data acquisition unit. A gas solenoid value switched between nitrogen and VOCs, allowing the difference in light transmission through the carbon dot film to be monitored, producing in a dynamic sensor signal.

First, we used our sensor system to detect vapor generated from passing nitrogen gas through hexane, acetone, ethanol, methanol, and water, which are commonly used in laboratories and industry. When vapor was flowed to the film, the sensor signal dropped as shown in Fig. 5a. This reflected a reduction in light transmission in the presence of vapors, compared with the reference nitrogen gas. When the flow was switched to the reference gas, the sensor signal returned to the original level. The gas cycles were repeated several times and the signals were reproducible. All vapors, with the exception of hexane, produced significant drop in sensor signal. Carbon dots are highly polar and this suggests that polar-polar interaction took place between the VOCs and carbon dots, leading to sensitive detection. The sensitivity of the film to VOC vapor was further quantified as gas sensing response using Eq. (3):
(3)
Gas
sensing response
S
=
I S
−
I R
I R
× 100
where I
S is the sensor signal to the VOC vapor and I
R is the signal to the reference gas (Table S2, Supplementary Information). As shown in Fig. 5b, the gas sensing response of the carbon dots using violet as a light source gave the highest response compared with other light sources. This is because carbon dots are strong absorber in the UV and violet regions. The gas sensing responses can be ranked as follows: water > ethanol > methanol > acetone > hexane. Different sensitivities are possibly due to the difference in interactions between the solvents and carbon dots, mainly governed by their polarities, which will be further investigated using the DFT methods. We further analyzed the sensor signals using principal component analysis (PCA). PCA is a statistical technique that reduces sets of data into score plots. A circle is used to mark the exclusive zone of each solution. Signals from all eight detectors were used as input data. We found that the PCA scope plots (Fig. 5c) were well separated with an excellent total variability of 99.8%, suggesting that our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose could differentiate these solvents and has potential uses in humidity, acetone, methanol, and ethanol vapor sensing. It can also be employed to determine the type of VOCs. Fig. 5c exhibits that acetone vapor produced the smallest area in the PCA score plot, suggesting that the signals from repeated cycles of acetone were the most reproducible.

Since our carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose produced the most reproducible signals for acetone vapor, we further investigated it when acetone/water solutions at different ratios (1, 5, 10, 25, 50, and 100% v/v) were used as the vapor sources (Fig. 6a). The sensor signal depended on the ratio of acetone to water in the solutions. It was found that as the amount of acetone decreased, the decrease in sensor signal became more pronounced. This was due to the strong reduction in light transmission by the water vapor. We found that none of the PCA score plots of the six acetone/water solutions overlapped with total variability of 99.9% even at 1% v/v acetone concentration (Fig. 6b). This means that our carbon dot-integrated electronic nose could be used to determine acetone in aqueous solutions with concentration as low as 1% v/v.

The capability of our nose system to detect acetone/ethanol solutions and to distinguish different concentrations was investigated (Fig. 6c). Five concentrations of acetone/ethanol solutions (0, 25, 50, 75, and 100% v/v) were used as the vapor source. The sensor signal decreased dramatically when the flow was switched to acetone/ethanol vapor. The sensitivity values increased as the ethanol concentration in the solution increased, which agreed with the above findings. The sensor signals were subjected to PCA. The PCA score plots (Fig. 6d) showed no overlap with a total variability of 91.3%, meaning that our sensor can be used to detect a vapor mixture between acetone and ethanol and to determine concentrations of acetone/ethanol as low as 10% v/v. These two are one of the most widely used solvents in the chemical, plastics, and fuel industries.

While extended studies of carbon dots have been previously reported, the computational investigation has often been inadequate, due to the complexity and variety in shape and size of such dots. In this study, we applied DFT methods to investigate the interactions between carbon dots and VOCs. As indicated by structural characterizations of carbon dots, the model of graphitic‑nitrogen-substituted graphene with oxygen-containing functional groups at the edge (C35H16N4O9) was optimized and converged to out-of-plane distorted geometry caused by nitrogen doping and edge functionalization (Fig. 7a). The solvent effects on the absorption spectra of the optimized carbon dots were then computationally studied using TD-DFT and PCM. The absorption spectra (Fig. 7b) were simulated from electronic transitions to the first 20 excited states in implicit solvents compared to those in gas phase [22]. The simulated absorption peaks were observed in UV and visible regions in a good agreement with our experimentally determined UV–vis absorption. This also validates our computational models. The absorption spectra of carbon dot model in bulk solvents exhibited the increase in extinction coefficients for all solvents studied when compared with the gas phase spectrum. The increase in extinction coefficients of carbon dots in various VOCs was consistent with the electronic nose results with the exception of hexane. The disagreement between the computational and electronic nose results for hexane in this case could arise from the TD-DFT calculations being based on the relative permittivity of the solvents rather than polarity. The polarity was found to have a negligible effect on absorption intensity, consistent with the report of Zhao et al. [59]. Relative permittivity and polarity are not necessarily mutually proportional.

To further investigate the interactions between carbon dots and VOCs, geometric optimization and calculations of interaction energies (I.E.) were performed. We used the larger graphitic fragment (C52H18N5O9) substituted in plane with nitrogen atoms and oxygen-containing groups, i.e. hydroxyl and carboxyl groups at the edge to better represent the carbon dots (Fig. 7c–f). The VOC molecules, comprising ethanol, methanol, acetone, and hexane, were placed on the basal plane of the carbon dot sheet. All optimized structures were again nonplanar because of the out-of-plane distortions caused by the substituted functional groups. The distances between VOCs and the carbon dot were in the range 3.31–4.10 nm, similar to those previously reported for alcohol-graphene systems [60]. From Table 1 , the I.E. values between carbon dots and VOCs can be ranked as follows: acetone > methanol > ethanol > hexane. The more negative the I.E. value, the stronger the intermolecular interaction, in other words the more stable the complex. These I.E. values suggested that interactions between VOCs and carbon dot were contributed by multiple factors, including polarity and C–H–π interaction. The interaction energy between the acetone and carbon dot was the lowest because acetone has lower polarity than the alcohols and less C–H–π interaction than the hexane. The contribution of the C–H–π interaction is consistent with the greater negativity of the I.E. value of the ethanol‑carbon dot complex, compared with the methanol‑carbon dot complex. This explains the electronic nose results, which showed that the carbon dots were most sensitive to ethanol, followed by methanol, then acetone. As shown in Fig. 7c and d, the OH groups and carbon backbones in the alcohols lay parallel in the carbon dot, indicating favorable polar and C–H–π interactions, consistent with the previous work [60]. The results thus demonstrate the importance of interfacial interactions between carbon dots and VOCs to explain the sensitive and selective detection towards strong adsorption of VOCs by the carbon dots.

The strong negativity of the I.E. value of hexane‑carbon dot complex was unexpected and indicated a strong C–H–π interaction between the hexane and our carbon dot. The optimized geometry of this complex showed that hexane molecule aligned parallel to the carbon dot with seven hydrogen atoms pointing towards the interface. The strong interaction between hexane and carbon dot model, however, contradicted the electronic nose results as no response was observed when hexane vapor was imparted to the carbon dot film. This contradiction was further evaluated by calculating the I.E. of the ethane‑carbon dot and propane‑carbon dot complexes. The I.E. values of ethane‑carbon dot and propane‑carbon dot complexes were −3.89 and −4.94 kcal mol−1, compared with −8.01 kcal mol−1 for hexane. The I.E. values were thus proportional to the number of interaction sites: three, four, and seven for ethane, propane, and hexane, respectively. The interaction site is referred to the number of hydrogen atoms interacting with carbon dot sheet. When the I.E. values were normalized by interaction sites, the values for those alkanes are similar (−1.14 kcal mol−1 for hexane, −1.30 kcal mol−1 for ethane, and −1.24 kcal mol−1 for propane). This suggested that the I.E. values of the nonpolar VOCs were governed by the size of the VOC molecules themselves. It is also noteworthy that the I.E. values of the methanol and ethanol complexes were lower than those of ethane and propane, despite the equivalent numbers of heavy atoms (C and O). This indicates the significant contribution by the polarity of the hydroxyl groups to the electronic coupling between the carbon dot and VOCs. We noted that the exceptionally strong interaction with hexane and carbon dot was attributed to hexane being the largest VOC in size in this study. Moreover, the absence of oxygen-containing functional groups allowed hexane access to the sp2 carbon-rich graphitic fragment, resulting in the lowest I.E. value. Although the DFT calculation and electronic nose results failed to agree well in this case, they were overall in good agreement for polar VOCs. We concluded that polarity and C–H–π interaction are the dominant factors governing the vapor sensing of carbon dots when used in an electronic nose. In addition, the absence of chemical bonding between the VOCs and carbon dot model suggested the physisorption phenomena, consistent with the observation that our carbon dot film continued to provide reproducible sensor signals after multiple reuse.

The calculation of interaction energies between the carbon dot and VOCs has led to crucial understanding and mechanistic insights into the sensing properties. We next used DFT methods to investigate the frontier molecular orbitals of the carbon dot, when complexed with acetone. Fig. 8a and b reveals that doping of the graphitic fragment with nitrogen in conjunction with edge functionalization decreased electron delocalization in the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), compared with the pristine graphitic sheet. This was due to the change in hybridization of carbon from sp2 to sp3 in the basal plane [62]. Fig. 8c shows the HOMO-LUMO of acetone‑carbon dot complex. Unexpectedly, the electron clouds were positioned only on the carbon dot sheet, but not on the acetone molecule. It was also observed that there was some transfer of electron clouds to the edge functional groups following excitation, as previously reported [63,64]. The presence of electron clouds exclusively on the carbon dot sheet confirmed the dominant electron-donating and accepting abilities of the carbon dot. Such ambipolar properties of carbon dots were reported by Kwon et al., who fabricated field-effect transistors using carbon dots as the semiconductor and observed p-type and n-type conduction mechanisms [65]. To verify that the carbon dots were indeed a strong electron donor and acceptor, we calculated their molecular orbitals of carbon dots when complexed with phenol, a more electron-rich molecule than acetone. Again, electron clouds were present only on the carbon dot sheet, confirming the strong electron donating and accepting abilities of the carbon dot (Fig. 8d). To further confirm this and the validity of our computational methods, HOMO and LUMO of phenol dimer were also calculated (Fig. 8e). As a result, the HOMO delocalized on one phenol suggesting a donor-like behavior whereas the LUMO delocalized on the other phenol suggesting an acceptor-like behavior. Our computational finding of HOMO and LUMO therefore confirms the ambipolar properties of carbon dots such that they could be potentially used as ambipolar semiconductors for various electronic and optoelectronic applications, such as light-emitting diodes, solar cells, energy storage devices, field-effect transistors, etc.

From these experimental and computational results, the unique structural and optical properties of carbon dots endow exceptional sensing capability towards VOCs. In the electronic nose experiments, acetone vapor was found to produce the most reproducible signals, producing the smallest PCA exclusive zone. To further demonstrate the versatility of the carbon dots, we next tested their ability to sense acetone in solution. Since the optical absorption of acetone interfered with optical absorption by the carbon dots, accurate results could not be obtained. We therefore tested the use of photoluminescence for sensing in solution phase. To do this, we dissolved carbon dots in acetone/water solution at different concentrations, filtered to remove residues, and measured the fluorescence intensities. As the concentration of acetone was increased, the fluorescence emission intensity also increased up to a concentration of 70% v/v, after which it dropped (Fig. 9 ). This decrease was due to the limited solubility of carbon dots in acetone. The results confirmed that the fluorescence emission of carbon dots also depends on the polarity of the medium. The limit of detection from the 3σ/slope method (σ = standard deviation) was 1.5% v/v of acetone to water, which was close to the electronic nose detection limit. Based on these results, our carbon dots have also proved to be capable of sensing acetone in solution.

The electronic nose and computational results illustrate the relationship between interfacial interactions between the carbon dots and VOCs and the sensing properties, as selectivity to different VOCs was due to differences in polarities and C–H–π interactions. The modulation of photoluminescence when the carbon dots were in acetone solution also amplifies the significance of the medium or surrounding environment. The electronic nose and photoluminescence results were also in a good agreement, as the optical absorption and photoluminescence of the carbon dots were enhanced in the presence of VOCs (Fig. 10 ). Although the solvent-dependence of optical properties of carbon dots have been previously reported [51,66,67], our work highlighted the significance of interfacial interactions and electronic coupling between the carbon dots and VOCs. The DFT calculations confirmed that these interfacial interactions and electronic coupling induced a change in the electronic state, and consequently a change in the optical properties of the carbon dots that can be observed using an electronic nose, UV–vis, and photoluminescence measurements.

In this work, we have described robust experimental and computational methods to investigate the VOC sensing properties of carbon dots. A simple hydrothermal method for synthesizing carbon dots from jackfruit was demonstrated. The carbon dots in solution were blue-emitting under UV excitation with a quantum yield of 5.2%. Their structural and optical properties were confirmed using several characterization techniques. The photostability of the carbon dots under UV excitation, ambient conditions, and in salt and aqueous solutions was excellent. The carbon dots were then used as a sensing layer in an optical electronic nose for real-time VOC sensing. With the aid of PCA, the sensor could differentiate the type of VOC. We further used our carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose to sense acetone vapor and distinguish different concentrations of acetone in aqueous and ethanolic mixtures. In addition to optical absorption, we have shown that the fluorescence emission of carbon dots can be used as a platform for acetone sensing in solution. The calculated interaction energies between carbon dots and VOCs suggest the potential of carbon dots as robust sensors and absorbents. This work has thus demonstrated the versatility of carbon dots derived from jackfruit as VOC sensors, which will have applications across several industries. Furthermore, the carbon dots were computationally found to show strong electron donating and accepting properties, proposing their potential uses in ambipolar electronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by Thammasat University Research Fund under the TU Research Scholar, Contract Nos. 5/2561 and 24/2561. The authors also acknowledge the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC), Thammasat University Center of Scientific Equipment for Advanced Research (TUCSEAR), Department of Chemistry, Department of Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University. N. Thongsai is supported by Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand. We acknowledge the partial use of computing resources from the Science Cloud Project at Department of Computer Science, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University as well as the National e-Science Infrastructure Consortium.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.04.269.
